heading: ELEVATA A UNA CONDIZIONE MIGLIORE
card_summary: This chunk opens the section "Raised to a Better Condition" in the Seventh Tone of the Third Canticle, treating the resurrection of the body as the culminating harmony of Zorzi's cosmological project. Working through five capitula (on the possibility, historical occurrence, rational demonstrability, inaugurating agent, and Scriptural testimony of resurrection), Zorzi draws on Ambrose, Pliny, Paul's letters to the Corinthians and Thessalonians, the Gospel of John, the Book of Job, the phoenix myth, kabbalistic traditions (the indestructible bone called "luz," the soul-term neshamah), Platonic-Plotinian arguments for soul immortality, and the Pharisaic doctrine of the world to come (olam ha-ba). The resurrection is framed throughout as a musical-cosmological return to "perfect consonance," in which the human body, purified of its gross and dissonant matter, is transformed into a spiritual, angelic form — the ultimate fulfilment of the De Harmonia Mundi's guiding metaphor.
essay:
## Note on Coverage

This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 99, which at 25,845 words is among the longest in the edition. The Read tool sampled approximately the first 600 lines (through p. 2228 of the Bompiani text), encompassing capitula I–V of the section. The remaining material (capitula VI onward, pp. 2228–2444) is not covered here.

---

## Central Argument: Resurrection as Perfect Harmonic Consonance

The heading "Elevata a una condizione migliore" — "Raised to a Better Condition" — announces the eschatological climax of the *De Harmonia Mundi*. The first sentence establishes the governing metaphor that threads the entire passage: God the supreme Artificer, who created the world with perfect harmony and governs it with a tranquil hand, will at the end restore it to "perfect and complete consonance in a better and perfect fate" (p. 2208). The human person, God's most accomplished work, will shed its "strident and dissonant matter" and cleave to the Artificer "with perfect melodies, to render due praise in harmony for ever." Resurrection is thus not merely a theological datum but the final movement of the cosmic symphony.

## Capitulum I: The Possibility of Resurrection (pp. 2208–2212)

Zorzi opens by citing the privileged cases of Enoch (Gen. 5:24), Elijah (2 Kgs. 2:11), and Moses as precursors of bodily transformation who did not undergo ordinary death. Moses's shining face (Exod. 34:29–35) is read as evidence that his body had already begun its transfiguration; hence neither corruption nor the devil's power touched his corpse, and the dispute between Michael and the devil over Moses's body (Epistle of Jude, v. 9) is glossed as the angelic prince claiming custody of a soul already translated to spirit. These few exceptional cases establish that the general resurrection is a transformation already begun in history.

The bulk of the capitulum answers sceptics who hold that what has been destroyed cannot return. Zorzi marshals a series of natural analogies:

- Bees generated from the carcass of a slain bull (within twenty days); scarabs from a horse's corpse — examples drawn ultimately from Virgil's *Georgics* and the ancient *bugonia* tradition.
- Annual plants (lettuce, cabbage, thyme, parsley) that dissolve into the earth yet revive from their seed each year.
- The phoenix, living more than five hundred years, self-immolating at the end of its life and rising from the ashes or marrow — Zorzi citing both Ambrose and Pliny as authorities.
- The silkworm's metamorphosis (larva → chrysalis → butterfly → eggs → larva again).
- The hardest nut (apricot kernel, date stone) generating the entire tree, which in turn yields seeds.

> *Ex corpore enim tauri fustibus caesi cellulaque reclusi ac humo sepulti, paucis, id est viginti diebus plus minus generantur apes.*

<cite>p. 2209 (Latin column)</cite>

The philosophical argument for resurrection's possibility turns on the persistence of form. If the form — and especially the soul — remains incorrupt (as "all true wise men" assert), then when it is reunited with its matter the same individual is reconstituted. Zorzi dismisses the Scholastic puzzle of the "third entity" (tertia entitas) — the debated question whether the resurrection body is strictly numerically identical with the pre-mortem body — as either non-existent or harmless to the doctrine.

### The Kabbalistic "Luz" Bone

In a passage of particular interest, Zorzi aligns the phoenix's regenerative marrow with the kabbalistic tradition of the *luz* bone:

> *hominis os quod ipsi* לוז *luz appellant, in magnitudine ciceris mundati, nulli igni, nullique corruptioni obnoxium, semper conservatur illaesum, ex quo (ut dicunt) repullulat totum corpus animale, veluti planta ex semine.*

<cite>p. 2212 (Latin column)</cite>

This indestructible coccygeal bone, which Zorzi attributes to "secretiores theologi" (the Kabbalists), is the bodily seed from which the entire resurrected body will sprout, just as a plant grows from a seed. He pairs it with the Hebrew term **neshamah** (נשמה) for the soul, and links both to the Platonic-Pauline doctrine that the risen body will be a "spiritual body" (*corpus spirituale*, 1 Cor. 15:44), shedding the gross feculence of the animal part. The risen person will be "transformed into an angel and into the very Son of God" — a formula that marks the highest aspiration of Zorzi's Christian-kabbalistic anthropology.

## Capitulum II: Has Resurrection Actually Occurred? (pp. 2212–2215)

Because resurrection is a contingent fact, it can only be established by testimony and empirical evidence. Zorzi enumerates the witnesses to Christ's resurrection: the apostles, the women at the tomb, the five hundred brothers mentioned by Paul (1 Cor. 15:6), and the crowds who witnessed the Ascension. The episode of Doubting Thomas receives extended commentary: Thomas's exclamation "My Lord and my God" (John 20:28) is read as the recognition that Christ's impassible body — accepting the touch of its own wounds without pain — is the pledge (*pignus*) of the transformation that awaits all believers. The angels' words both at the empty tomb and at the Ascension are cited as celestial corroboration.

Paul's argument in 1 Cor. 15 that without resurrection Christians would be "more wretched than all other creatures" (since they abstain from earthly pleasures to gain a reward they would never receive) is presented as a rational moral demonstration of the doctrine's necessity.

## Capitulum III: Can Resurrection Be Proved by Reason? (pp. 2215–2222)

Zorzi takes as his methodological epigraph a Platonic caution: divine things are to be received by oracle rather than demonstrated by human ingenuity. Yet since the oracles teach nothing contrary to nature, it is legitimate — indeed the "most elegant kind of knowing" — to show how revealed truth accords with purified intellect.

The rational proof proceeds from the immortality of the soul. Zorzi argues:

1. The soul participates in divine and eternal light, as do the angels; Plato and Plotinus establish that whatever God creates *immediately* (without secondary cause or pre-existing matter) is eternal.
2. Everything that participates in a nature shares in its properties: since angels are incorruptible and immortal, so is the soul which shares in their light and origin.
3. The soul therefore cannot be perfected while weighed down by the corruptible body; it must achieve its end — full participation in God — in a transformed, glorified body.

> *Est itaque anima haec immortalis, et perpetua, ut perpetuo frui possit sempiterno Deo, et ipsius particeps esse, a cuius consortio peregrina et si aliquando per negligentiam decidit, nec plene et pure suscipit ipsum, in se tamen habet seminaria quaedam, unde favente ipso superno sole reparare ac revocare possit compressas vires.*

<cite>p. 2220 (Latin column)</cite>

The eschatological communion is described in terms of the mutual banquet of Apocalypse 3:20: Christ is simultaneously the food and the fellow-diner, the "living bread" (*panis vivus*) consumed by the redeemed and the one who is nourished by them — a figure of mystical reciprocity rooted in John 6 and the Eucharistic tradition.

Paul's trumpet-call in 1 Cor. 15:51–53 ("in momento, in ictu oculi, in novissima tuba") is quoted at length as the clearest Scriptural disclosure of the resurrection's mystery. An astrological argument — that resurrection could be demonstrated by showing planetary positions to be identical to those at the world's creation — is dismissed as invalid: human bodies were not all created at the same moment but over many successive centuries, so no single celestial configuration can serve as the resurrection's trigger.

## Capitulum IV: Who Inaugurated Resurrection and How Fittingly (pp. 2222–2226)

Paul's proclamation of Christ as "first-fruits of those who have fallen asleep" (1 Cor. 15:20–22) structures this section. The typological contrast of Adam (earth-man, "living soul") and Christ (heavenly man, "life-giving spirit") organises the anthropology: from the first Adam humanity has its tendency back to dust (Gen. 2:7, 3:19); from the second Adam it has its capacity for ascent to heaven. Gabriel's annunciation (Luke 1:35) is read as the inauguration of the second creation. The abolition of death — understood as both physical dissolution and the person of the devil as "angel of death" and "prince of this corruptible world" — is seen as the victory definitively won at the Crucifixion ("when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself," John 12:32) and to be fully implemented at the Last Day.

## Capitulum V: The Testimony of Scripture (pp. 2226–2228)

Job 19:25–26 ("I know that my Redeemer lives, and on the last day I shall rise from the earth, and again I shall be surrounded by my skin, and in my flesh I shall see God") is cited as the clearest Old Testament prophecy, with Zorzi glossing "in my flesh" as referring not to the present corruptible flesh but to flesh "transformed to a better condition." Martha's confession in John 11:24 and Paul's extended treatment in 1 Cor. 15 and 1 Thessalonians are aligned as mutually confirming testimonies from the same "academy" (Zorzi's recurrent metaphor for the tradition of divine wisdom). The key Pauline formulas — "it is sown an animal body, it rises a spiritual body; sown in ignobility, rises in glory; sown in corruption, rises in incorruption" — are quoted and then linked to Christ's own promise in Luke 20:36 that the resurrected "will be like angels of God." Zorzi resists the Scholastic view (attributed to "more recent theologians") that risen humans differ *specifically* from angels, arguing that the Pauline and Gospel texts point to a genuine angelic transformation, not a merely accidental resemblance.

## Key Sources and Technical Terms

- **Scripture**: Gen. 5:24 (Enoch); 2 Kgs. 2:11 (Elijah); Exod. 34 (Moses); Jude 9 (dispute over Moses's body); 1 Cor. 15 (Paul on resurrection); Luke 20:36; John 11:24 (Martha); Job 19:25–26; Apoc. 3:20.
- **Patristic**: Ambrose (on the phoenix and resurrection harmony); Augustine (angels at the font of divine light); Hierocles (crimes extinguish faith in a judge — a Neoplatonist cited alongside the Fathers).
- **Classical/natural history**: Pliny (*Natural History*, on the phoenix); the bugonia tradition (bees from bulls).
- **Philosophical**: Plato (divine truths received by oracle); Plotinus (confirming the eternity of what is immediately created).
- **Kabbalistic**: *luz* (לוז) — the indestructible resurrection bone; *neshamah* (נשמה) — the higher soul; *parash* (פרש) — "separated" (etymology of Pharisee); "secretiores theologi" as Zorzi's standard designation for Kabbalistic sources.
- **Hebrew**: the Pharisees (*perushim*, "separated ones") as bearers of resurrection doctrine, cited via Paul's self-identification in Acts 23:6; the *olam ha-ba* (world to come) literature.

## Connection to the Larger Cosmological Project

The section brings the *De Harmonia Mundi*'s governing musical metaphor to its eschatological conclusion: all creation will return to the "perfect consonance" from which it descended, but now in a condition superior to the original. The kabbalistic *luz* bone and the Platonic *seminaria* (seed-powers latent in the fallen soul) converge to image a resurrection that is simultaneously natural renovation and supernatural grace. The typology of two Adams secures the Christocentric framework within which Zorzi's syncretic Platonism, Kabbalism, and Christian theology are held together.
